title: NOC 84110 – Chain saw and skidder operators | GTR Immigration
description: Chain saw and skidder operators under NOC 84110 are the frontline technical workers of Canada’s forestry and logging industry. As a TEER 4 occupation, these professionals are responsible for the manual felling of trees and the mechanical transport of timber from the forest floor to loading areas. In 2026, while much of the industry has shifted to automated harvesters, the need for skilled chain saw operators remains critical for steep-slope logging, selective harvesting, and wildfire mitigation projects. For immigration, this role is a strong candidate for the Atlantic Immigration Program (AIP) and the BC PNP Entry-Level and Semi-Skilled stream.

classification.teer: Classification LevelDetailsBroad Occupation Category8 – Natural resources, agriculture and related production occupationsTEER Level4Major Group84 – Workers in natural resources, agriculture and related productionSub-Major Group841 – Workers in natural resources, agriculture and related productionMinor Group8411 – Chain saw and skidder operatorsNOC Code84110

duties: Felling: Use chain saws to fell trees, ensuring they fall in a safe direction to avoid damaging other timber or equipment.
Processing: Trim limbs and tops from felled trees (delimbing) and saw them into specific lengths (bucking) based on mill requirements.
Skidding: Drive and operate skidders (cable or grapple) to pull logs from the felling site to a central landing area or "yard."
Maintenance: Perform routine maintenance on chain saws and skidders, including sharpening chains, lubricating parts, and making minor field repairs.
Safety: Adhere to strict forest safety regulations, including the use of personal protective equipment (PPE) and maintaining safe distances between crew members.
Sorting: Help sort logs by species and size at the landing area to prepare them for truck transport.
employment_requirements: Completion of secondary school is usually required.
Work Experience: Several months of experience as a logging or forestry laborer are typically mandatory.
On-the-Job Training: Formal training of up to several months is provided by the employer.

Certifications: * A provincial Chain Saw Operator's Certificate or Faller Certification (compulsory in BC and some other provinces).First Aid Certification is mandatory for all workers in remote woodland operations.
First Aid Certification is mandatory for all workers in remote woodland operations.
A valid driver's license (often Class 5 or 3) is usually required to operate skidders on forest roads.
Exceptional physical stamina and the ability to work in all weather conditions are essential.
salary_prospects: Note: Experienced fallers in B.C. working on complex terrain often earn higher piece-rate wages that can significantly exceed the median hourly estimates.
job_outlook: The outlook is Favourable. While mechanical harvesting has replaced some manual roles, the demand for specialized "Hand Fallers" is increasing in areas where machinery cannot safely operate. Furthermore, the retirement of the "Boomer" generation of loggers is creating a localized labor gap in many of Canada's timber-dependent communities.
labour_market_demand: The demand for chain saw and skidder operators is Good, particularly in British Columbia, New Brunswick, and Northern Ontario. In 2026, there is a specific surge in demand for operators to support Canada’s "Climate Resilient Forests" initiative, which requires selective thinning and brush clearing to prevent forest fires. As many veteran operators move into supervisory roles, Canadian logging contractors are actively seeking international workers with strong physical stamina and mechanical aptitude.
Essential Industry Status: Forestry is a multi-billion dollar cornerstone of the Canadian economy, providing high job security in rural and northern communities.
Technological Hybridity: Gain experience operating modern, GPS-equipped skidders while mastering traditional manual felling techniques.
Outdoor Lifestyle: Ideal for those who enjoy working in Canada’s pristine wilderness and diverse natural landscapes.
PR Strategic Pathway: A recognized occupation for several "Semi-Skilled" provincial streams that offer a direct bridge to Permanent Residency.
pathways: BC PNP Entry-Level and Semi-Skilled (ELSS): A high-priority route for those working in the forestry sector for at least 9 consecutive months in BC.
Atlantic Immigration Program (AIP): A direct pathway for workers with job offers from designated employers in the Atlantic provinces (NS, NB, PEI, NL).
New 2026 TR to PR Pathway: A temporary measure targeting 33,000 workers already in Canada, with priority for "Essential Resources" like forestry.
Rural and Northern Immigration Pilot (RNIP): Accessible in specific forestry-dependent communities across Ontario and Western Canada.
